comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of  John Winthrop Jr. (1606-1676), one of the first alchemists of colonial America, member of the Royal Society, and important figure in the founding of the Connecticut colony. The next time someone acts as though occultism consists solely of what was created in England between the founding of the Golden Dawn in 1887 and whenever Gerald Gardner finished penning his first Book of Shadows, I want you to think of this gentleman bending over an alchemical athanor and then checking a few lines in the writings of Hermes Trismegistus. 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 354,219th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

Hello!

What are witch's bottles?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: witch's bottles
replies.content: Bottles containing urine and certain other things -- recipes vary -- that are buried under a house to protect the people in it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Many thanks for your reply!  Do they have any major drawbacks compared to other forms of magical protection?  It seems to me like a big choice to commit a personal concern to a crawlspace, a personal concern that could very well out live it's maker!  And all of that negative energy trapped in the basement!  It seems like making and deploying a witch's bottle could be dicey, and I'm curious if my concerns are justified or if I'm overthinking it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You're kind of overthinking it. Witch bottles do tolerably often outlive their makers -- in fact there's a fine book by Ralph Merrifield, The Archaeology of Ritual and Magic, that talks among other things about witch-bottles that turn up centuries later under old British houses. The negative energy doesn't stay put, though -- it diffuses into the earth, where it's composted like anything else. (There's an exact etheric equivalent of the decay processes that yield compost.) You hide it in the crawlspace or what have you precisely to keep anyone else from finding and using what is, as you've noted, a potent personal concern.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Oh yeah, they work.

comments.title: hypnosis and will
comments.content: I don't get hypnosis. People, in response to simple, boring commands or suggestions, seem to surrender their free will. From an occult perspective what is happening there? And how does it relate to will?

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
Well, first of all, most people don't have much free will to surrender. What we call "waking consciousness" is little more than a shallow doze; that's why it's necessary to tackle the hard work of meditation to wake up and get around to noticing the difference between your inner world and your outer world (to use the labels we've been discussing over on the blog). Thus it's fairly easy for someone who's learned the trick to push people a little further into a doze, and get them to confuse what the hypnotist is saying with the products of their own minds.

(Television also does that, by the way. That's one of the reasons why throwing your TV into the dumpster is such a useful magical discipline!)

In the Golden Dawn tradition, and in quite a few other occult schools as well, it's a standard rule that initiates should never practice hypnosis or allow it to be practiced on them; many of the more ethical schools won't permit a professional hypnotist to join. The reason's quite straightforward: hypnosis works by making people less conscious; the ethical occultist seeks by contrast to make them more conscious, to wake them up to their potentials for conscious freedom and choice.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
Thanks, very interesting. I read that both Freud and Jung started with hypnosis but abandoned it.

Would self-hypnosis also be ill-advised, say using recorded audio self-hypnosis to help with bladder control, a problem my partner has? Her need to pee seems to be an irrational, unconscious, spiteful(?) urge, that occurs in certain situations, especially knowing that a toilet is near but being unable to make it that far. Is there a better way to help problems like this using meditation or other occult practices?

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content: Err, bladder problems like that can have a strong psychosomatic component, with links to sexual issues and letting go... would looking into these issues be an option, instead of hypnotically suppressing what the body wants to tell?

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
Very much so, and for the reason another commenter has already brought up: self-hypnosis for something like that can sometimes paper over the symptoms but it doesn't deal with the causes. For that, your partner needs to get down to the emotional roots of the difficulty. In cases like this, those usually have to do with emotional trauma left over from the experience of being toilet trained. (Freud has been mocked mercilessly for his focus on this, but he was quite correct that it's an issue, and it was even more of an issue in the upper-middle-class Victorian Jewish circles where so many of his early patients came from.) Journaling, as described in the Magic Monday FAQ, is one way some people find helpful in this sort of thing; you might also see if you can get her remembering and talking about any related experiences in childhood, as sharing such things with another person can be very healing.

BTW, when you get a situation where a physical urge seems to be acting spitefully, very often what's going on is that the person has gotten in the habit of treating her body the way that she was treated in childhood by a dysfunctional parent. The body then acts out the anger the child couldn't express at the time. Yeah, it's as problematic as it sounds.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
This makes a certain amount of sense of my situation now that you've brought it out. I smoked cigarettes for 13 years and then went to a hypnotist to quit. Worked like a charm (why is that?). I walked out of the guy's door and didn't smoke for 7 years...until I had that run-in with the psycho dude in '13. Then I smoked a pipe for 2 more years. When I was ready to quit again I called the hypnotist for a tuneup. He informed me that he was out of the business and despite my flattery refused to help.

But I quit anyway. I figured I had every intention of quitting with his help, surely I could do it without it. And I did. I returned the old family pipe I'd been smoking to my Grandfather and haven't smoked tobacco since.

I love smoking pot though. Not habitually, but about as often as I can manage the thing. When I have some I smoke it like I smoked tobacco: first thing in the morning, and periodically throughout the day. Obviously this doesn't lead to the most productive days. I know this. So I don't buy it very often. I just smoke when it's offered, and chip in a little on my friends' bags so I'm not a total scrub. That sort of self-control I have.

What I want to know is, now that I've been successfully hypnotyzed (successful enough that I could tap into it again 9 years later) how do I undo that influence?

And secondly, is the pot smoking keeping me from really being done with it? I love smoking pot, but know I need to keep it sparse for my own good. Might it just be something I have to learn to live without in order to be done with the tobacco? Or, might I be able to develop a healthy relationship with the herb over the long haul? (That would be my ideal scenario!)

Desperately,
Tripp

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, relax. Sit down, take a deep breath, have a beer if you like. I doubt that you tapped into the hypnosis nine years later; there's a thing called will, and if you have enough of it, you don't need hypnosis.

Second, if you like smoking pot, and it doesn't interfere with your life, why are you worrying about it? The Puritan notion that anything you enjoy must be eeeeeeeeeevil, and must be cast aside so that you can be pure and miserable and fit for heaven, was a bad idea even when it was first invented and it hasn't improved any since then. If you choose to smoke pot, and can manage the habit in a way that doesn't inconvenience anyone else, it's no more of an issue than, oh, if you choose to enjoy Sriracha sauce or what have you.

Pleasure isn't morally bad, any more than it's morally good. How you handle your pleasures can be a moral issue, but that's another matter, of course.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
LOL. Thank you. Beer in hand. That was just what I needed. I definitely have some will power with my Leo-Leo-Leo self! I guess the hypnotist was just a placebo. At least for Round 2.

I still wish I could develop a healthier relationship with cannabis though. Any advice on how to progress from impulsive smoker to being more in control? I have no desire whatsoever to quit forever.

And is there anything I can do to wash that hypnotic gray right outta my hair? Discursive meditation perhaps??

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
That hypnotic treatment was a long time ago; by now, not one atom currently making up part of your body was there when you received it, so don't worry about it.

Regular practice of the usual magical disciplines will build strength and clarity of will and make it easier for you to find a place of balance.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
I know this is way late, and I don't expect any kind of a reply, but this is one of those fallacies that irks me enough to speak up.

I keep hearing the thing about everything in your body is replaced every 7 years, and it simply isn't true. In particular, DNA is not replaced. It's copied and repaired, but it is not replaced. The poster child for this is a woman's egg cells: they're created early on (I think before birth), and don't change until they're used, 40 or more years later. Brain cells are another and much more globally relevant.

John Roth

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content: Thanks John and others. Good food for thought/meditation and suggestions.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content: But then... even in meditation, how can you really be sure if your thoughts are your own thoughts?

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content: They aren't. The task you face is developing the degree of mental independence and insight at which you can actually think your own thoughts -- and that, in turn, is what discursive meditation does.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content:
If one thinks one never had original thoughts, in the sense of "Many a man fails as an original thinker simply because his memory is too good.", does that mean some relevant difference from others regarding the uses of discursive meditation?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: hypnosis and will
replies.content: Nope. It's by meditative practice and similar forms of work that you become able to have original thoughts, whether or not you think you have had any before.

comments.title: The Planes of Magic
comments.content:
Hi JMG

Have read Mystery Teachings and now working through DMH.

On which plane does magic operate?

I am up to the practices of Transform Yourself and Transform the World. I guess the first works on the Etheric plane. But when we take magic outside ourselves, are we working on a different plane?

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content:
It depends on what kind of magic you're doing, of course. Most of what's in The Druid Magic Handbook works on the astral plane, the plane human beings experience by way of the imagination. Mental magic works with the basic patterns of thought -- not concrete thoughts (which are words and images, and thus astral) but the structures of meaning underlying them, the thing that (for example) connects the spoken or written word "orange" to the sensation of color. Etheric magic works mostly with the breath, on the one hand, and with etherically active material substances such as herbs on the other. Physical plane magic? That's science and engineering. Spiritual plane magic? That's enlightenment and mystical experience. You can do any and all of them...

But again, most of what's in The Druid Magic Handbook works on the astral plane, as that's the usual plane where ceremonial magic works.

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content:
Great, thanks. A follow up, if I may.

Natural Magic then works on the Etheric plane (herbs etc)? Or can it work on the Astral plane too?

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content: Natural magic works on the etheric plane. You can supplement it with astral working by adding in visualizations, verbal spells, or what have you, but that's added on to the basic work of natural magic.

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content:
That looks like you just described natural magic, ceremonial magic, mysticism, and hard science. If that makes sense, does "mental magic" correspond to something else as directly?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content: Discursive meditation!

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content: Who'd have imagined that? Thanks.

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content: That’s fascinating... so the Buddhist practice of Guru Yoga - imagining an enlightened teacher or Buddha in front of you after color breathing and the like, then merging yourself with that image to attain clear enlightened sight of the world - would operate on all top four levels, from etheric to spiritual?

replies.title: Re: The Planes of Magic
replies.content: Yes, and that's why it's designed the way it is. The Buddhist approach is usually to move upward from the physical -- so you start with asana and mudra, move from there to breathing, from there to visualization, from there to meditation, and from there to a mystical state if you can manage it. Other traditions do things in other ways, of course.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I generally recommend self study these days. There are still some good groups out there, but there are many more that range from dubious to awful, and the good ones generally don't advertise themselves.

As for where to go next, well, first, I trust you're planning to finish Learning Ritual Magic before you do anything else. That's a basic skill in magic, and the more scrupulously you finish what you start, the more you'll get out of your training.

After that, I'd encourage you to spend a little while considering the options. My books on Druidry and the Druid-themed GD work are among those, but they're certainly not the only game in town. One caution -- a lot of people outside the British Isles tend to think that "Celtic" means "Irish," and of course it's much more complex than that; the material in The Celtic Golden Dawn is from Welsh rather than Irish sources, and the two are emphatically not the same. (Linguistically and culturally, the difference between the Welsh and the Irish is about on a level as the difference between the English and the Germans.)

On the other hand, ethnic origins don't actually matter that much in magic; I've known first-rate Druids whose ancestors came from Africa and China, equally good Cabalistic ceremonial magicians whose ancestors were Irish, Taoists whose ancestors came from Europe, and so on. So once you finish your current course of study, look around, try a ritual here and a ritual there, and see what appeals to you before you begin your next course of study.

comments.title: Exiting Magical Practice
comments.content: JMG - I am not planning on exiting magical practice. But if one were to leave a regular magical practice, is there some sort of procedure for doing so safely and effectively?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
Nope. Once you're on the path, you're on the path. Browning's lines from "Childe Roland" are apropos:

"For mark! no sooner was I fairly found
Pledged to the plain, after a pace or two,
Than, pausing to throw backward a last view
O’er the safe road, ’t was gone; gray plain all round:
Nothing but plain to the horizon’s bound.
I might go on; nought else remain’d to do."

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
Hmm. Ok. So then, are there risks with just stopping practice?

Using your analogy of the SOP being the spiritual equivalent of a good shower, I guess things could get pretty funky. But apart from that, are there other risks now that the road has been crossed?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: It's simpler than that. Once you make a good start on magical practice, the shell has broken and you're out of the egg, and there's no way back into it. You remain in contact with the magical dimensions of reality; if you give up practice, you simply get rid of the things that enable you to deal with those dimensions constructively, and become their plaything.

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
How does that work out with the need to put practice on hiatus with an infant?

BoysMom

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: That's one of the reasons why natural magic is so widely used around infants -- it's safe for them, and it's a good way to protect the mother if she's been involved in magical practice. As the child grows, she can resume ceremonial practice in stages. Since giving birth to and raising a child is a major magical action -- one of the most potent workings there is, in fact -- it's not really "time off," just doing the work in a different way.

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
That sounds frightening. You've said before that magical practice involves danger, but I didn't realize there was a no-going-back clause.

I originally started reading your magical writings because of what seemed to be an encounter with an unknown entity several years ago that seriously spooked me. I thought then that defensive practices of some kind would be worthwhile, whether the danger was real or illusory. But by crossing the line into magical practice, did I thereby make the danger real?

To clarify, I've been doing daily tarot divination for 16 months and near-daily discursive meditation on the Cosmic Doctrine for 8 months (since you started that series on the blog), and have found it helpful in focusing my awareness throughout the day and deepening my thinking. Does this mean I'm 'out of the shell' and can't go back?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
No, the danger was real all along. You were in the magical world all the time anyway, and vulnerable to the forces of that world -- but there's a difference between being wholly unconscious of that world and therefore of interest to only a few of its beings, and being at least partly conscious of it and of interest to many more.

My question to you is, do you want to go back into the shell?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
Thanks for bearing with me on this; it seems important that I get this right.

I take your response to imply that yes, after more than a year of Tarot practice and eight months of discursive meditation on the Cosmic Doctrine I am out of the shell. Am I right?

One way to counter the unwanted interest of beings in the magical world without going deeper into magical practice might be to take refuge in the protection of a deity. I have the impression that it happens fairly often that people renounce initial forays into occultism and embrace Jesus instead. Do you think this would work?

To address your question, although 'going back into the shell' has its appeal, I would rather take the adventure. I'm taking the next five months to travel and reflect on whether to pursue further the Qabalistic path I've been contemplating over the last year, or another that has more endorsement by an exoteric religious community- Christian mysticism, Yoga, and nature spirituality all speak to me as well. I was planning to suspend my discursive meditation practice during my travels and perhaps tarot divination as well. I thought this would be a period of pause and sober discernment, but would I actually be putting myself at greater risk by suspending my practices?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
1) As I noted above, when you're "out of the shell" depends on subtle personal factors I can't gauge over the internet.

2) Yes, that's an option.

3) Since you haven't taken up ritual work, and discursive meditation is simply a deepening of ordinary thinking, you should be fine.

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: Thank you! This was very helpful to have clarified.

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: What’s your take on the Wiccan-to-Christian stories where they say all is well?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: A lot of people don't do Wicca as a serious magical practice; it's a religion, and changing one religion for another is common enough. Of course if you're a convert to Christianity you kind of have to say that everything's just fine and the love of Christ has made your life wonderful, since that's what your new religion says ought to be happening...but I'm sure it happens that way at least some of the time.

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content:
How much work before you are 'on the path'?

Particularly if your first time performing the sphere of protection or LBRP produced a rather profound effect... Same goes for divination readings...

If you followed one of these courses for a few months and then broke off the practice for one reason or another, are you on the path?

...or does beginner success imply the path was taken in a past incarnation, and I have little choice in the matter?

replies.title: Re: Exiting Magical Practice
replies.content: It's very common for students to have the burst of success at the beginning. As for when the process becomes irreversible, that seems to vary from person to person. If you get bored shortly after trying it and drop the practice, then you're not on the path yet, but once you keep up regular practice for a while -- and how long varies from person to person -- you're out of the egg, to switch metaphors, and in a much wider world.

comments.title: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
comments.content:
Last month you helped me understand why my 2-year-old perceives people as "being" or having colors. I understand from you that clairvoyance is common in young children, but also that my daughter may have a "considerable" capacity for it (evidenced by her already perceiving emotional patterns as well as the basic tone.)
Over the last weeks I've had much to process, and often started drafting "Magic Monday" questions, only to realize that getting my answers is going to be a journey, or that the questions were better directed to the diety I serve.

I've now landed on a quandry I hope is appropriate to bring to this forum.
My daughter is very sensitive to others' emotions, even if not directed at her. I notice it in the way it influences her behavior.
My family situation is fraught with emotion. Most significant is that my father (who is in his mid-50s and lives in another state) has incureable, but currently stable, cancer; his wife died quite suddenly and unexpectedly 3 months ago; and their two children are still quite young: ages 7 and 14.
Naturally, I find this situation emotionally distressing, but I cope with it well enough. However, when I am talking to a caring friend about this matter, or any unhappy matter, despite all my outward calm and composure, my child becomes agitated, starts acting out to distract me, and if I persist in my conversation anyway, will whine and yell: "Dont talk about! You MAY NOT talk about!" (She doesnt specify about "what", but the "what" is clearly any topic that causes me distress.) Since it affects her so much, I now avoid talking or even thinking too much about unhappy things.(I figure this is an untenable long term solution and that suppressing my emotion is not healthy for me, but for the moment it's the bandaid I'm using.)
Also, my daughter sleeps in the same bed as me, and has since infancy. If I lay awake silently ruminating on upsetting things, it disturbs her. She will flail or even wake up.
1. Is there advice/resources you or your readers can offer, in how to support a clairvoyent child faced with stressful stimuli?
2. Is it possible to do something that could turn down the intensity for her (and if it is possible, is it adviseable)?
3. Are there coping mechanisms that could be applied or taught to a not quite 2.5 year old? She is quite bright for her age but still very much a little child.
4. Am I being excessively concerned? She is a toddler, and many things greatly upset a toddler but are not harmful to them (being told "no" is the principle example here).

Relevant observations/information-
Things that have helped: Long hugs, Singing, Diversions, especially humorous ones (saying a silly nonsense phrase and laughing), Rhyming

Things that dont seem to work at this time: Deep breaths, counting, talking/reasoning

Also- shifts in attitudes/worldviews notwithstanding- because of vows I have made, the following are things I cannot participate in, neither directly nor via proxy: divination, astrology, ritual magic. I would include things like Sphere of Protection/invoking/banishing in the list of things I have barred myself from doing.

I dont at this point feel comfortable with meditation outside of the way I do it for my personal spiritual practice. (Which seems at least superficially similar to discursive meditation. I dont understand discursive meditation enough to truly compare it to what I do)

I apologize for bringing up so many limits, but I feel it's important that I honor my vows until/unless my God tells me I'm free of them.

I am inclined to think that natural magic is fine. Prayer is a very comfortable practice for me.

Thank you again for this opportunity to ask questions.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content:
I'm far from an expert in childraising, not having done it in this life (or any of my last half dozen), so please take the following with a grain of salt.

Your daughter is going to have to learn to cope with her clairvoyance, and that's going to involve some significant discipline, i.e., not interrupting Mommy in the middle of a conversation just because it makes her feel uncomfortable. Two years isn't too early to start that process. You can't shield her from her gifts, but you can help her learn the self-discipline she'll need to make them an asset to her and others, rather than a liability. Yes, that's going to involve some meltdowns, along the lines of those elicited by the word "No."

I gather from what you've said that your own spiritual practice is based in one of the Abrahamic religions. (And whether or not that's the case, if you've accepted a religious discipline and taken vows, of course you should follow them to the letter.) You might explore what resources your own tradition has to offer; for example, if you belong to one of the branches of Christianity that work with saints, you might see if there's a suitable saint to whom you can turn for help.

Those are the things that come to mind just now.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content: If I may ask, is there any particular reason you haven't had kids in your last few lives?

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content: A lot of souls do that when they're focusing on wrapping up things on the human level and moving on. It keeps the burden of karmic ties from piling up any further than necessary.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content:
I waited until your reply before bringing this up. I'll offer it tentatively.

If the two year old is still sharing a bed with her mother, their etheric bodies and auras are touching and sharing energy all night long. Might that not make the child more sensitive than average to her mother's emotional states during waking hours? If that is causing problems for both of them, perhaps gradually weaning the child from the habit of sharing the parent's bed (a difficult thing to do, I'm sure) might help the child to psychically separate herself. The mother has to be willing, of course, for this to work.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content: That's a valid concern. Still, one change at a time is good for a child that age; first work on teaching some self-discipline, and after that's established, get her out from the maternal energy coccoon.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content: Not the poster, but as someone who works with kids, for someone who's never had kids your advice is always remarkably good.

replies.title: Re: Supporting a clairvoyant 2 year old
replies.content:
Thank you, Deborah. That seems like a useful suggestion, and I'm definitely willing to give it a try. John, does it strike you as reasonable to first separate our spaces and energy, and then focus on her self-discipline when her emotional input from me is likely less intense?
Regarding self-discipline: since she is already well disciplined in other areas, I dont find it daunting to apply it in this area too. I had chosen avoidance of triggers over enforcing a disciplined manner because I was unsure if the latter was a reasonable expectation. Processing her own emotions seems quite a large enough task for a little one, without also having to deal with the emotions of people around her.
By way of analogy, I have a young autistic nephew, and he may not be able to behave appropriately in certain environments. Yet from my (admitedly limited) understanding that is is not a function of his lacking discipline but of sensory overload and the way his brain is wired.
On the other hand, I understand discipline does help with emotional regulation. She is realizing, for example, that while she may feel like whining or screaming, that doesnt get her the desired results, and so she finds more appropriate ways to express her desires. (I'm still working on helping her *gracefully* accept that sometimes the answer is simply going to be "no" regardless of how appropriately she expresses said desires.) She is also currently able to realize and articulate when her whining is a result of fatigue. ("Mommy I'm being tired!" she tearfully told me the other day. She then asked to have a nap.)
I was approaching her clairvoyance like a condition that required the avoidance of unpleasant triggers. I was seeking to shield her, and afraid of the damage that may be caused by failing to do so.
It seems that is unnecessary. And instead I can and should be focused on equipping her to deal with the additional perceptions right along with everything else.

So, breaking the co-sleeping habit may well not help you with regard to that problem. Just a data point for you. Best of luck with this!

comments.title: Nature's attitude towards humans.
comments.content:
Hi JMG and others

I would like to hear if other people are having a particular experience. Several times now when trying to commune with nature, and trying to gauge the attitude of a part of nature towards humans, I got a response like "they're annoying but they'll be gone soon". I'm not sure if it's humans or the annoying actions that will be gone soon.

Has anyone else had similar experiences and/or any insights? I guess it would vary with location a lot though. This was in Christchurch NZ.

Thanks all.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: Yes! I have a plot of land in the mountain, I inherited from my father, where I have been planting trees for the last 20 years, and to which I had been devoting lots of affection only intervening to plant and protect from forest fires in the summer. Since last year the place have been telling me to let go, not with hostility but I don't fill as welcomed anymore...

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: It does vary with location. Here in North America there are places where it's felt very clear to me that humans will be around for the long term, and other places where it feels just as clear to me that in the not too distant future there will be cracked lumps of concrete and a few other signs of our passing, and not much else.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content:
In your experience is it possible to get any idea of a time frame for these kinds of perceptions? I do get the sense that the land views 100 years as not very long.

I also got a vision in my minds eye of the river I was talking to dried out with a cracked bed. It felt like this was linked to there being very few humans around. Climate change is going to be one hell of a sucker punch if that's anything to go by. It reminds me of omens you talked about happening for native Americans prior to being nearly wiped out.
Do you get similar experiences to that?

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content:
It's very hard to do that with most nonhuman beings, as their sense of time is very different from ours -- not just a different scale, but a completely different understanding.

One of the reasons I'm living east of the Mississippi and north of Dixie is that I do indeed get experiences like that.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: The stand of trees I've worked with regularly for about 20 years, clearing vines, planting,thinning etc. have been unusually grouchy and short tempered this winter and early spring. We are in the central Midwest US hardwoods connected to Yellowood Forest that is being heavily harvested right now, which probably answers the question locally. All of the woods and forests in our area have been devastated by one pest or another for at least ten years. They are pretty sick of we humans just now and won't miss us when we are gone.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: Yes; in 2011 while walking along a highway and hitchhiking in North Carolina, the forests alongside the road gifted me a vision in which they advanced over the asphalt, and raised the temperature to that of a fever, and I saw humans being "sweated out," by disease and hunger, reduced to a tiny bands of hunter gatherers who eked out meager sustenance in a reality very much akin to that of a fever dream.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: Yes.  Esus showed me the Danada forest preserve (area in suburban Chicagoland where I take walks) covered by a shallow ocean.  "From ocean it came, to ocean it will return" was approximately what he said about it.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content:
I've gotten something similar when I've been in the northeast Texas/Arkansas pine belt: I'm not sure the land is hostile to humans per se, but my impression is that it's really not happy with the American South and would very much prefer that Dixie's culture be ripped out root, stem, and branch. I'm not sure whether that's the South's long tradition of extractive plantation agriculture or that the South made the mistake of running its slave economy in a part of the world where you really, REALLY shouldn't do that*, but either way I suspect that even more than the rest of America that part of the South is staring down the barrel of a very ugly reckoning.

(The I-20 corridor from Monroe to Meridian, meanwhile, is a spiritual dead zone and I'm not sure what's up with that. I've had the same sense in two other places, but in both of those it made sense - Huntsville, Texas has Texas death row, Biloxi's next to the Gulf dead zone - and AFAIK there's no such obvious reason for that corridor to be the way it is. In theory Mississippi River pollution would fit, but Baton Rouge seemed unaffected and so did Memphis the one time I passed through...)

* - I would not be surprised if in at least parts of North America slavery is the highest sin.

replies.title: Re: Nature's attitude towards humans.
replies.content: Could the I-20 dead zone have begun with the crucial Battle of Vicksburg in 1863?  Monroe and Meridian are about the same distance, in opposite directions, from Vicksburg.  Such things can last, IMHO, for centuries.

comments.title: Goetic entities/ Children and magic
comments.content:
1. From your book Circles of Power:

"Finally, many of the older grimoires and a few books that are more recent discuss the evocation of demons. Here the best advice is a single word: don't. " Do you still agree with this? What are your thoughts on the work of Jake Stratton Kent et al who believe these beings are less evil and Qlipothic and more sub-lunary or terrestrial spirits (that is, they belong to a different hierarchy than the celestial, they are closer to the earth and therefore more useful for material matters although perhaps a little wild)?

2. Are there any dangers to a young child occasionally being in the vicinity of a place where magic is practised (Goetic or otherwise)?

3. I know that in your FAQ that you suggest pregnant mothers and parents of children under the age of 3 avoid most magic, but what are your thoughts on them (parents or children) purchasing and wearing consecrated talismans of, say, planetary powers?

Obrigado!

replies.title: Re: Goetic entities/ Children and magic
replies.content:
1) My advice remains the same. Yes, I'm aware that demonolatry has become very fashionable of late, and it's not my job to tell people what to do unless they ask me for my advice; Kent et al. are grownups and can make their own life choices -- but my advice remains the same. I also don't agree that the other hierarchies are any less potent on the material plane; I've had excellent results working with elemental and planetary intelligences, as well as with angels -- it's simply that they have ethical concerns, and won't get whatever you want just because you want it. Since ethics are as essential to magic as sanitation is to surgery -- in either case, if you neglect that, things go septic in a hurry -- that's not a disadvantage.

2) No young child should ever spend time in a place where spirits are summoned. Do you really want the child to end up obsessed or possessed?

3) Depends on what you mean by planetary powers. Angels and gods, sure. Others? I'd advise against it.

[IMAGE:0]

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I should probably put this in the FAQ, it comes up often enough.

According to the Druid teachings I received, the order of the first aicme was BLNVS -- Fearn is pronounced with a V sound -- because that spells out the name of BeLeNVS, Belenus, the old Druid sun god. When Ireland was converted to Christianity, the order was changed to BLVSN to shed that bit of pagan tradition. My book uses the older form, the Murrays' deck the newer one. Both work -- and in fact I've used the Murray's deck for years, with very good results. So don't worry about it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If I may, I have one more question about the Ogham cards - I guess one would have to shuffle them not only like a deck of cards, but also turn some of them randomly from top to bottom in order to avoid have the same cards always coming up reversed? Sorry for the very basic question.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: With any kind of divination cards that assigns meanings to reversals, you have to shuffle in such a way that new cards get reversed and un-reversed with every shuffle. The usual trick is this: when you separate the pack into two halves, before shuffling them together, turn one of them -- just one, not both -- so that the heads are where the feet were and vice versa. Then shuffle them together. Split the pack, turn, shuffle; split, turn, shuffle, and you've thoroughly randomized which are upright and which are reversed. Does that make sense?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, that makes perfect sense. Thanks again!

Dear Archdruid,

replies.title: Re: Old Sources
replies.content:

replies.title: Re: just my imagination
replies.content:
Testimony retrieved by hypnosis is no longer accepted as valid in the courts, because when the mind's in a hypnotic state it confabulates -- that's a fancy word for "makes stuff up." For the same reason, I don't recommend hypnotic regression as a way of accessing past lives. Yes, the only safe way to get at past life memories is to pursue spiritual practice and let them surface when they're ready to surface; and yes, because it always involves decreasing your level of consciousness (and usually involves giving over some degree of control over your thinking to someone else), hypnosis is discouraged by most reputable occult schools.

If you pay somebody to put a curse on a third person, they get the raspberry jam effect but you get the karma. Protective measures will help, but check out how many people who do lots of nasty magic end up shunned, sick, lonely and miserable.

Not JMG, but Ian Wilson was able to trace a goodly number of past lives to the person, under hypnosis, regurgitating and jazzing up historical fiction that the person had genuinely forgotten encountering. What seemed to he happening was that the hypnotist began asking for past lives, some hypnotists pressing quite insistently, and the hypnotized person would duly come up with something. This would happen even if the person didn’t care about past lives, just volunteered for the experiment for fun. The phenomenon is called “cryptomnesia.”

Therefore, probably a first step in determining the truth of past lives is to try to remember if you have read any history, fiction or fact, in which one of your past lives lived. With luck, there won’t be anyone well known to history such as Cleopatra VII—who hasn’t at least heard of her? (Well, maybe some young Americans...). On the other hand, if you remember being someone from a fairly obscure civilization like the Scythians, there’s more of a reason to entertain tge hypithesis that the past life might have been real. Good luck with your research!

comments.title: Hermes Trismegistus
comments.content: Who or what is or was Hermes Trismegistus?

replies.title: Re: Hermes Trismegistus
replies.content:
Okay, once upon a time there was a Greek god named Hermes, and an Egyptian god named Thoth. One of Thoth's titles works out into English as "three times great." After Alexander the Great conquered Egypt and one of his generals established a Greek-speaking kingdom there, the Greeks who became the new Egyptian upper class did as people typically did in those days, and identified their gods with the local gods; thus, to them, Hermes = Thoth.

Fast forward a few centuries, and you've got a hybrid Greek-Egyptian intellectual class who are busy mixing Greek philosophy with Egyptian magic to produce most of the basic ideas of modern occultism. It was a common habit in those days, if you were writing mystical philosophy, not to put your own name on what you wrote -- that was tacky; instead, you put the name of some great figure of the past into the author's byline as a gesture of respect. (Some of the books of the Bible got their supposed authors that way.) So various authors producing books of magical philosophy all attributed them to Thoth, who was the god of magic among other things. Since they all wrote in Greek, though, they called him Hermes, but they put the Greek version of that title "three times great" at the end of the name; in Greek, "thrice greatest" works out to Trismegistus. So you had all these books -- the Corpus Hermeticum or "body of work of Hermes" -- credited to Hermes Trismegistus, who was then taken by later authors to be a historical figure.

comments.title: Scrying in the spirit vision and writing fiction

Dear John Michael Greer,

Thank you for hosting these Magic Monday question and answer sessions.

I was struck by a comment you made last week about "scrying in the spirit vision" and writing fiction. It has always seemed to me that a lot more is going on when writing fiction than most creative writing literature and workshop leaders would recognize, never mind acknowledge. I also suspect that this sense that something more is going on, and specifically, on the astral plane, is, at least to some degree, behind the procrastination many writers fall into after their first efforts in writing fiction.

I am familiar with the creativity literature by such authors as Julia Cameron, which aknowledges Muses, angels, and so on, but I had not come across "scrying in the spirit vision."

I would be curious to know any further thoughts on scrying in the spirit vision and how it relates to writing fiction that you might be willing to share.

replies.title: Re: Scrying in the spirit vision and writing fiction
replies.content:
It's a complex issue, and has a lot to do with the nature of imagination. If you practice imagining, according to occult philosophy, you're literally learning how to perceive and shape the astral plane, and the more vivid you make your imagined scenes, the more your astral senses develop.

Here's an exercise for you to try: imagine that in front of you is a door, and beyond the door is the living room of a character you're trying to understand. Imagine yourself standing up, going to the door, and knocking politely. The character answers the door, welcomes you in, waves you to a seat, and offers whatever beverage that character would likely have on hand. Now, all in your imagination, have a conversation with the character; ask questions, listen to the answers, and don't be surprised if the character says things you never expected him or her to say!

That's scrying in the spirit vision as a creativity exercise. You'll find that it makes your characters far more vivid and original.

The crucial rule is this: in scrying in the spirit vision, you are co-creating the experience with astral forms and beings. You are not in charge, and neither are they; you're working with each other to create something bigger than both of you. Give it a try, and watch where it leads...

replies.title: Re: daylight savings time

comments.title: precognition
comments.content:
This is a comment rather than a question. The March 4th New Yorker has an article on scientific examination of precognition. "The Psychiatrist Who Believed People Could Tell the Future." by Sam Knight. It is quite interesting. It also discusses such things as "voodoo death," and mentions the condition you wrote about in _Monsters_ in which Hmong refugees were dying of unexplained causes.

It seems clear to me that precognition could be useful in small, close knit communities. If I have a reputation for accurate prediction and one morning I say to you, "John Michael, don't go on that elk hunt today, I have a bad feeling about it." you would be likely to find something else to do. You might never know what danger you escaped, but if the gifts were more frequent in one community than another that group would have a survival advantage.

But in our larger culture, even if everyone believed in precognition the appropriate action would be difficult to determine and execute. One of the examples was a foreseeing of a plane with 124-maybe 125, people crashing in the hills. Without a flight number, or a serial number or the name of one or more of the victims, how could anyone use that information? You can't ground every flight that carries 125 people, or every flight that is going into hilly territory. A plane did crash with 124 immediate fatalities and 2 later deaths. The plane was flying into an airport surrounded by hills and crashed after circling in bad weather. So, good prediction, but so futile.

Another example in the article was a man who dreamed of a machine spelling out Aberfan, which was the Welsh town in which the collapse of coal slag buried a school, killing over a hundred children. It must have been terrible for him to live with, knowing that he had had a key piece of information that, combined with other precognitions could have saved lives, but didn't.

Rita

replies.title: Re: precognition
replies.content: Precognition also helps the individual who has it. I've several times been stopped from doing something that turned out, unbeknownst to me, to be much riskier than I had any way of knowing.

Psychic radar is also good for slowing down just before the speed trap. :)

On the other hand I once participated in a ritual with sort of sketchy planning--nothing really evil, just a little transgressive. In the month after it I had three auto malfunctions. Nothing dangerous, but expensive and annoying.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hi Violet,

My astrological study has mainly been in the Hellenistic tradition(s), but there, at least, there are a few things that may be relevant to your question.

(1) In addition to sign rulerships (e.g. Sun in Leo), we also have the exaltations of each planet, which seem to have an effect throughout the sign, but are especially prominent at a particular degree. The planets are in their depression at the opposite signs/degrees. The standard table for exaltations is:

Sun 19° Aries
Moon 3° Taurus
Mercury 15° Virgo
Venus 27° Pisces
Mars 28° Capricorn
Jupiter 15° Cancer
Saturn 21° Libra

(2) The bounds (known to the medieval astrologers as "terms," from the Latin word terminus, meaning "end-point" or "limit") divide each sign of the zodiac into five unequal segments, each of which is ruled by one of the classical planets other than the luminaries. Adding this rulership to the sign-based rulerships will provide depth and nuance. Given how arbitrary the degree assignments are, though, it may not quite qualify as "a basic rule of thumb."

(3) Again, the system of decans (10° divisions of the zodiac) will provide another overlay. Here, 0°-10° Aries is assigned to Mars, then continue in descending Chaldean order, so 10°-20° Aries is the Sun, 20°-30° Aries is Venus, 0°-10° Taurus is Mercury, then Moon, Saturn, Jupiter, Mars... Again, it provides an overlay that can give depth to the rulerships. Given the sequential nature of this, it could stand to be a rule that's as "basic" as the zodiacal rulerships themselves.

FWIW, the Hellenistic astrologers knew about both, but really seem to emphasize the the bounds/terms, while the decans really come into their own in later centuries. I'm still at the stage in my own studies where I'm observing what's effective in reading various charts, and don't have a considered view yet.

Hope this helps, or at least provides some material for further reflection and research!
